revision.text:
[[bn:বীজ (ভারতীয় দর্শন)]][[en:Bīja]][[es:Bījā]][[fa:بیجا مانترا]][[fr:Bīja]][[id:Bija]][[it:Bīja (concetto)]][[ja:種子 (唯識)]][[kn:ಬೀಜ (ಸಂಸ್ಕೃತ ಪದ)]][[nl:Bija]][[pt:Bija]][[ru:Биджа (мантра)]][[uk:Біджа]][[vi:Chủng tử]][[zh:一切種子識]] {{Short description|Sanskrit term meaning "seed"|qq=}} In {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hinduism|1=Hinduism}}, {{ill|Jainism|en|Jainism|qid=Q9232|lt=Jainism|1=Jainism}} and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, the {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} term '''Bīja''' ({{lang|sa|बीज|qq=}}) ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Jp|1=Japanese language}}. 種子 shuji) ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} 種子 zhǒng zǐ), literally {{ill|Seed|en|Seed|qid=Q40763|lt=Seed|1=Seed}}, is used as a metaphor for the origin or cause of things and cognate with {{ill|Bindu (symbol)|en|Bindu (symbol)|qid=Q2302600|lt=bindu|1=Bindu (symbol)}}. [[File:Om siddham2.png|thumb|right|100px|The Om bija in [[:en:Esoteric Buddhism|Esoteric Buddhism]]]] ==Buddhist theory of karmic seeds== Various schools of Buddhist thought held that karmic effects arose out of seeds that were latent in an individual's {{ill|Mindstream|en|Mindstream|qid=Q6863985|lt=Mindstream|1=Mindstream}} or psycho-physical continuum.Fukuda, Takumi. Bhadanta Rama: A Sautrantika before Vasubandhu, Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies, 26 (2), 2003. Rupert Gethin describes the theory thus:
When I perform an action motivated by greed, it plants a 'seed' in the series of [[Dharma#Buddhism|dharmas]] [phenomena] that is my mind. Such a seed is not a thing in itself - a dharma but merely the modification or 'perfuming' of the subsequent flow of dharmas consequent upon the action. In the course of time this modification matures and issues in a particular result, in the same way as a seed does not produce its fruit immediately, but only after the 'modifications' of the shoot, stem, leaf, and flower.Gethin, Rupert. The Foundations of Buddhism, page 222.
The [[:en:Sautrantika|Sautrantika]] school held such a theory as did the [[:en:Mahasamghikas|Mahasamghikas]] and the early Mahasisakas.Lamotte; Pruden. Karmasiddhiprakarana, 1987, page 28. The Sautrantika Sthavira Srilata held a conception of "subsidiary element" (''anudhatu'' or *''purvanudhatu'') which also corresponds to this theory of seeds. The seed theory was defended by the Buddhist philosopher {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|qid=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu|1=Vasubandhu}} in his [[:en:Abhidharmakosha|Abhidharmakosha]] who mentions that is the view of the “old teachers” (''purvacarya''). It is also present in the ''Viniscayasamgrahani'' of the [[:en:Yogacarabhumi|Yogacarabhumi]].Kritzer, Robert. Sautrantika in the Abhidharmabhasya In the Bashyam Vasubandhu connects the Sautrantika theory of seeds with the notion of the latent defilements or [[anusaya]]:
The Sautrantikas define [[anusaya]]s as {{ill|Kleshas (Buddhism)|en|Kleshas (Buddhism)|qid=Q19969527|lt=kleshas|1=Kleshas (Buddhism)}} in the state of seeds and say that they are not separate {{ill|Dravya|en|Dravya|qid=Q5305972|lt=Dravya|1=Dravya}}s (substances). Anusayas are dormant, i.e., not actualized, while paryavasthanas (active defilements) are awakened.Kritzer, Robert. Sautrantika in the Abhidharmabhasya, p. 364
Likewise, the ''Nyayanusara'' of Sanghabhadra states that the theory had different terms to refer to "seeds":
There are certain masters who give different names to these seeds, each according to his own understanding. Some call them subsidiary elements (''anudhatu''), others call them impressions (''vasana''); still others call them capability (''samarthya''), non-disappearance (''avipranasa''), or accumulation (''upacaya'').
The theory is considerably extended in the [[:en:Consciousness-only|Consciousness-only]] teachings of the [[:en:Yogacara|Yogacara]] school of Buddhism. According to this theory, all experiences and actions produce ''bīja'' as impressions, stored in the [[:en:alaya|alaya]] (storehouse) consciousness. The external world is produced when the seeds "perfume" this consciousness. ==As Mantras== {{Main|Bījamantra|qq=}} In {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} Buddhism and {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hinduism|1=Hinduism}}, the term ''bīja'' is used for mystical "seed syllables" contained within {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}}s or standalone seed syllable mantras ({{ill|Bījamantra|en|Bījamantra|qid=Q85746282|lt=bijamantra|1=Bījamantra}}). These seeds do not have specific linguistic meaning nor are they name mantras, but they may stand for specific principles, deities, powers, or ideas.Khanna, Madhu (2003). ''Yantra: The Tantric Symbol of Cosmic Unity. '' Inner Traditions. {{ISBN|0-89281-132-3|qq=}} & {{ISBN|978-0-89281-132-8|qq=}}. p.21 The best-known ''bīja'' syllable is [[Om]], first found in the Hindu scriptures the {{ill|Upanishads|en|Upanishads|qid=Q6113985|lt=Upanishads|1=Upanishads}}. In Buddhism, the most important seed syllable is {{ill|A in Buddhism|en|A in Buddhism|qid=Q113298140|lt=the letter A bija|1=A in Buddhism}}. In some tantric traditions, the Bija of the '[[Varnamala (disambiguation)|Varnamala]]' (Sanskrit; English: "garland of letters"; which may be rendered as alphabet) are understood as aniconic representations and sound embodiments of the {{ill|Matrikas|en|Matrikas|qid=Q1789401|lt=Matrikas|1=Matrikas}} (a group of goddesses). In {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}} the seed syllables corresponding to the [[trikaya]] are: a white ''oṃ'' (enlightened body), a red ''āḥ'' (enlightened speech) and a blue ''hūṃ'' (enlightened mind).{{cite book|last=Rinpoche|first=Pabongka|title=Liberation in the Palm of Your Hand: A Concise Discourse on the Path to Enlightenment|publisher=Wisdom Books|year=1997|pages=196|qq=}} In the [[:en:Bön|Bön]] tradition of Tibet, it's a little different: a white ''āḥ'', a red ''oṃ'' and a blue ''hūṃ''. Bijas are often the vehicle of [[:en:Esoteric transmission|Esoteric transmission]] of [[:en:Terma (Buddhism)|terma]] to a '{{ill|Tertön|en|Tertön|qid=Q2493637|lt=Tertön|1=Tertön}}' (Tibetan; English: "revealer of terma"), such as that experienced by {{ill|Dudjom Lingpa|en|Dudjom Lingpa|qid=Q1271624|lt=Dudjom Lingpa|1=Dudjom Lingpa}}.*Lingpa, Dudjom; Tulku, Chagdud; Norbu, Padma Drimed; Barron, Richard (Lama Chökyi Nyima, translator); Fairclough, Susanne (translator) (1994, 2002 revised). ''Buddhahood without meditation: a visionary account known as 'Refining one's perception' (Nang-jang)'' (English; Tibetan: ''ran bźin rdzogs pa chen po'i ranźal mnon du byed pa'i gdams pa zab gsan sñin po''). Revised Edition. Junction City, CA, USA: Padma Publishing. {{ISBN|1-881847-33-0|qq=}} p.xx ==Cross-cultural correlates== {{ill|Guruwari|en|Guruwari|qid=Q5620577|lt=Guruwari|1=Guruwari}} of the [[:en:Indigenous Australian|Indigenous Australian]] peoples is an interesting cross cultural correlate and may be cognate. See also {{ill|Gankyil|en|Gankyil|qid=Q3758127|lt=Gankyil|1=Gankyil}} of the {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} tradition which is cognate with {{ill|Bindu (symbol)|en|Bindu (symbol)|qid=Q2302600|lt=bindu|1=Bindu (symbol)}}. In the respected fieldwork published in ''Aboriginal Men of High Degree'', [[:en:A.P. Elkin|A.P. Elkin]] cites what he in his professional opinion is evidence that traders from {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}} brought fleeting contact of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} and {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hinduism|1=Hinduism}} to areas near modern-day {{ill|Dampier, Western Australia|en|Dampier, Western Australia|qid=Q1158897|lt=Dampier|1=Dampier, Western Australia}}.[[:en:Elkin, A.P.|Elkin, A.P.]] ''Aboriginal Men of High Degree: Initiation and Sorcery in the World's Oldest Tradition''. 1973. [[:en:Inner Traditions|Inner Traditions]], 1993. {{ISBN|978-0892814213|qq=}} Traditions of [[:en:Mantrayana|Mantrayana]] were also evident in Indonesia, e.g. [[:en:Candi Sukuh|Candi Sukuh]]. And it is in the Vajrayana and Mantrayana traditions of [[:en:Esoteric transmission|Esoteric transmission]] where bija take precedence. Indeed, bija defines Mantrayana. Elkin interpreted a link between {{ill|Indigenous Australians|en|Indigenous Australians|qid=Q170355|lt=Indigenous Australian|1=Indigenous Australians}} culture and Buddhist ideas such as {{ill|Reincarnation|en|Reincarnation|qid=Q128593|lt=Reincarnation|1=Reincarnation}}. He argued this link could have been brought through contact with Macassan traders. ==See also== * {{ill|Bindu (symbol)|en|Bindu (symbol)|qid=Q2302600|lt=Bindu|1=Bindu (symbol)}} * {{ill|Gankyil|en|Gankyil|qid=Q3758127|lt=Gankyil|1=Gankyil}} * [[:en:Surya Namaskar|Surya Namaskar]] - the Sun Salutation, in which each posture is sometimes associated with a Bījā mantra ==References== {{reflist|30em|qq=}} {{Buddhism topics|qq=}} [[en:Bīja]] [[bn:বীজ (ভারতীয় দর্শন)]] [[es:Bījā]] [[fa:بیجا مانترا]] [[fr:Bīja]] [[id:Bija]] [[it:Bīja (concetto)]] [[ja:種子 (唯識)]] [[kn:ಬೀಜ (ಸಂಸ್ಕೃತ ಪದ)]] [[nl:Bija]] [[pt:Bija]] [[ru:Биджа (мантра)]] [[uk:Біджа]] [[vi:Chủng tử]] [[zh:一切種子識]] {{wikidata link|Q2416043}} [[Category:Buddhist philosophical concepts]] [[Category:Hindu philosophical concepts]] [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Metaphors]] [[Category:Yogacara]]
